title: Clinopodium breviflorum
taxonomy: Clinopodium breviflorum is classified within the family Lamiaceae, commonly known as the mint family. This classification is based on its botanical characteristics and genetic relationships with other plants in the Lamiaceae family. More specifically, Clinopodium breviflorum is a member of the tribe Mentheae, a group of plants known for their fragrant leaves and small flowers. Within the tribe Mentheae, Clinopodium breviflorum is placed in the subtribe Mentinae, a subcategory of plants that are often shrubby or herbaceous in habit. The classification of Clinopodium breviflorum reflects its evolutionary relationships with other plants in the Lamiaceae family.
uses: Clinopodium breviflorum is reported to be used in traditional medicine in various parts of the world, including Asia and Latin America. Its leaves and stems are used to treat a range of health issues, from fever and colds to digestive problems and skin conditions. In some cultures, it is believed to have anti-inflammatory and antiseptic properties, which may help to soothe wounds and reduce pain. However, more research is needed to confirm its effectiveness and potential side effects. Further studies are necessary to fully understand the medicinal uses and benefits of Clinopodium breviflorum.

intro: Clinopodium breviflorum, also known as Short-flowered Clinopodium or Short-flowered Calamint, is a perennial herb belonging to the mint family (Lamiaceae). Traditionally, Clinopodium breviflorum has been used for its medicinal properties, including its anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial effects, which can help alleviate respiratory issues and soothe digestive problems. The bioactive constituents of this plant include volatile oils, flavonoids, and phenolic acids, which are responsible for its medicinal properties. In herbal medicine, Clinopodium breviflorum is commonly prepared as teas, infusions, or tinctures to utilize its health benefits, particularly in the form of a calming and decongestant tea.
